text: COMPTON-DANDO, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 2 m. E by N of Pensford. Area 1,210 acres. Pop., including the hamlet of Tucking Mills, 359.
COMPTON-DUNDON, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 24 m. N of Somerton, N of the river Carey. Area 2,790 acres. Pop. 679.
COMPTON-DURVILLE, a tything in the p. of South Petherton, co. of Somerset, 12 m. NW of South Petherton. Pop. 136.
COMPTON-FENNY, a parish in the co. of Warwick, 5½ m. E of Kington. Area 2,330 acres. Pop. 615.
COMPTON-GIFFORD, a tything in the p. of Charles-the-Martyr, co. of Devon, 12 m. NNE of Plymouth. Pop. 271.
COMPTON-GREENFIELD, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 6 m. NNW of Bristol, at the mouth of the Severn. Area 640 acres. Pop. 65.
COMPTON-LITTLE, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 41 m. NW by W of Chipping-Norton. Area 1,670 acres. Pop. 301.
COMPTON-LONG, a parish including the hamlet of Weston, in Warwickshire, 4 m. NNW of Chipping-Norton. Area 3,530 acres. Pop. 829.
COMPTON-MARTIN, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 7 m. N of Wells. Area 2,260 acres. Pop. 300.
COMPTON-NETHER, a parish in Dorsetshire, 2; m. W by N. of Sherborne. Area 1,390 acres. Pop. 225.
COMPTON-OVER, a parish in Dorsetshire, 32 m. W of Sherborne. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 72.
COMPTON-PAUNCEFOOT, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 5 m. W by S of Wincanton. Area 870 acres. Pop. 256.
COMPTON-VALLANCE, a parish in Dorsetshire, 7 m. WNW of Dorchester. Area 2,310 acres. Pop. 116.
COMPTON-WYNIATES, an extra-parochial liberty in the co. of Warwick, 44 m. ENE of Shipstonupon-Stour. Area 930 acres. Pop. 46. CON

COMRIE, a parish in Perthshire, consisting of the strath at the head of Strathearn and of Lochearnside. Pop. in 1801, 2,458; in 1841, 2,471. The parish contains the villages of Comrie, Dalginross, Ross, and St. Fillan's.—The village of C. is finely situated on the 1. or N bank of the Earn, 61 m. W of Crieff. Pop. 803. It enjoys the unenviable distinction of being more frequently visited by perceptible earthquakes than any other spot in the British islands. The greatest shock ever experienced here occurred on 23d October, 1839. It was slightly perceptible over a great part of Scotland, but nowhere so distinctly as at C.
CON. See CONN.
CONA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 24 m. SW of Venice.
CONABURY, a village in the p. of Monasteroris, King's co. Pop. 162.
CONAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Mirambeau, 12 m. WSW of Jouzac. Pop. 1,598.
CONADILLY, a river of NE Australia, which falls into the Nammoy, on the l. bank, in S lat. 31° 7'. Its head-streams rise in Liverpool plains; and the general course of the river is NNW.

CONAN, a river in Ross-shire, which rises in Loch Chroisk, in the p. of Contin, about 35 m. NW of its mouth. It first flows E through Strathbran; and after receiving the Gradie from Loch- Fannich, flows into Loch-Luichart; issuing thence, it flows in a SE direction, and receives the Meig flowing from Loch Benachan; 5 m. below this it receives, on the N side, the Garve, which rises on the confines of Lochcross, and flows ESE; 2 m. E of its junction with the Garve, while sweeping round the finely-wooded grounds of Castle-Brachan, it receives the Orrin from the SW; and then turning N, at Conan house, flows into the W extremity of the frith of Cromarty. Its breadth at its mouth is about 50 yards, but it is comparatively shallow here, although throughout much of its course it is a deep dark-coloured stream. The C. is a fine troutingstream, and there are valuable salmon-fisheries upon it. The river-mussel, Mya margaritifera of Linnæus, occurs in the C.; and fine pearls have occasionally been obtained in this river.

CONAN (BRIDGE OF), a village 2½ m. S of Dingwall, in Ross-shire, so named from a stone-bridge of 5 arches, with a water-way of 265 ft., here erected over the Conan in 1809, over which the road from Beauly to Dingwall is carried.

CONANICUT, an island off the coast of Rhode Island, U. S. It is about 8 m. in length, and 1 m. broad. Its S end, called the Beaver's Tail, forms the W point of Rhode Island harbour. There is a lighthouse, with a fixed light, on the Beaver's Tail, in N lat. 41° 26′ 50″, W long. 71° 24′ 25″.
CONCA, a village of Corsica, recently founded on the E coast, near Porto Favone. Pop. 500.

CO'NCAN, an extensive maritime district of Southern India, in the prov. of Bejapore, extending along the shore from Damaun to Malabar, between the parallels of 16° and 20°; bounded on the E by the Western Ghauts, which separate it from Ahmednuggur, Punah, and the district of Sattara; on the S by Canara and Sattara; on the W by the Indian ocean; and on the N separated from Calliani by the small river Sawatri. It may be estimated at about 220 m. in length, and from 40 m. to 60 m. in breadth. Its area is estimated at 12,270 sq. m. The surface is rugged, and is watered by numerous mountainstreams, but by no river of any magnitude. Towards the Ghauts it is deeply intersected by ravines, and covered with thick forests; and the passes on its E frontier are seldom practicable for wheeled carriages. CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND The coast presents diversified landscapes of an undulated country near the beach, with a back-ground formed by the Ghauts, and at long intervals white dwellings amid green groves of cocoas. From Zyghur on the sea-coast, to the Ambal pass, there is a rich hilly country, capable of cultivation, with a considerable pop. Beyond this, the mountains rise to the height of 4,000 ft., and are of difficult ascent. The products of this country are the same as those of Malabar; the hemp raised is said to be of a stronger quality than that raised above the Ghauts. The sugarcane, turmeric, and ginger, are grown in the S districts. The coast has a very straight general outline, but is broken into shallow bays and harbours. These, with the uninterrupted view along the shore, the elevated coast favourable to a sweeping view of the horizon, and the land and sea-breezes, which, blowing alternately in the 24 hours, force vessels steering along the coast to be always within sight of it, have rendered the coasts of C. from time immemorial the seat of piracy; and so formidable were these piratical depredators in the 18th cent., that all ships found it necessary to receive a pass from the chiefs of the pirates. The Great Mogul maintained a fleet for the express purpose of checking them, and they were frequently attacked by the Portuguese. In 1756, their principal port and fortress, called Gariah, was taken by a British squadron, and their fleet destroyed. The pop. in 1829 was estimated at 1,044,120; and the number of native schools was reported at the same period to be 419, with 9,399 scholars. The rugged districts along the side and base of the Ghauts are chiefly inhabited by Bhilli; the lower and S districts are chiefly inhabited by Coolies. The Brahmins of this prov. are a peculiar race, not acknowledged in the rest of India. A peculiar language is used in C., closely connected with the Sanscrit. The revenue of North C. in 1830 was £106,590; of South C. £105,550. The principal towns are Calliani, Gorah, Jaghur, and Gariah.—According to ancient traditions, this country was inhabited by a tribe of savages, till they were conquered by the Hindus, who gave it to a tribe of Brahmins, and it was held by them until taken possession of by the Mahommedan kings of Bejapore. It was conquered in the 17th cent. by Sivā-ji, the founder of the Mahratta empire. Towards the close of that cent., Kanho-ji Angria established a kingdom on this coast, extending 120 m. from Tamanah to Bancoote, together with the inland country as far back as the mountains. The dominion of this prince and his family continued for nearly 70 years, when it was put an end to in 1756 by the allied British and Mahratta forces, the former under Admiral Watson and Colonel Clive, and the country, with the exception of Bancoote, was restored to the Peishwa. The N part of the country, from 18° 50' to 20° 20' N lat., was ceded to the British in 1817; and the whole was definitively taken possession of by the British in 1818. When ceded, almost every hill had its own fortification, and every rock of an inaccessible nature had been converted into a fortress. All these are now rapidly crumbling into decay.

CONCARNEAU, a canton, commune, and maritime town of France, in the dep. of Finistère, arrond. of Quimper. The cant. comprises 4 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,056; in 1841, 7,600. The town is situated on an island in the bay of La Forêt, at the mouth of the Moreau, 14 m. SE of Quimper. Pop. in 1846, 2,000. It is surrounded by old walls, and defended by a fort. The harbour is safe, and affords good accommodation to small vessels, of which 300 are annually employed in the adjacent fisheries. The trade consists principally in grain, wine, cider, salt, wood, and the produce of the fisheries. Of the pilchard alone as many as 30,000 barrels have been exported in a single season. CON638 This town was taken in 1373 by Duguesclin, and in 1576 by the Protestant leaguers.

CONCEIÇAO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 350 m. NNE of Goyaz, and 60 m. SSW of Natividate, 10 m. N of the junction of the Rio Palma with the Parannan. Pop. 2,000, chiefly agricultural.—Also a village and small port in the prov. of Alagoas, district of Poxim, on the l. bank of the Cururipe, about 21 m. from the sea.—Also a parish in the prov. of Para, dio. of the Rio Negro, on the N side of Lake Canuma, 50 m. N of Serpa. Its inhabitants are chiefly agriculturists.—Also a town in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, parish of Santo-Antonio-de-Sá, on the Macacú.—Also a town in the same prov., in the parish of Cantagallo.—Also a small town in the prov. of Bahia and district of Jaguaripe.—Also an isolated mountain of the same prov., 5 m. NE of Cachoeira. It is 8 m. in circumf. at the base.—Also a town in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, district of Caheté, 110 m. NNE of Mariana.—Also a small town of the prov. of Maranhão, in the comarca and 50 m. SW of Caxias, and about 10 m. from the l. bank of the Parnahiba, noted as the scene of a defeat of the rebels in 1840. -Also a missionary establishment in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, on a small river of the same name, 15 m. above its confluence with the Baure, an affluent of the Guaporé. It is inhabited by Indians of the Cabixi tribe.—Also a town of the prov. of Parahiba, in the district of Souza.—Also a small town in the same prov., 40 m. from Villa-Real-de-São-Joao, in a mountainous locality. Its inhabitants are chiefly employed in agriculture. See also RIO-DE-JANEIRO and ITANHAEN.
CONCEICAO, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, in the district of São-Joâo-d'Elrei.

CONCEIÇAO-DA-BARRA-DE-SAO-MATHEOS, or BARRA, a town and district of Brazil, in the prov. of Espirito-Santo, on the r. bank and at the mouth of the Oricare or São-Matheos, 8 m. from São- Matheos, and separated from the prov. of Bahia on the N by the Rio Mucuri.

CONCEIÇAO-DA-LAGOA, a parish of Brazil, in the centre of the island of Santa Catharina, 10 m. NNE of Desterro. Pop. 3,000, chiefly agricultural. The church stands on a hill, and commands a view of the sea, although distant upwards of a league. The parish contains numerous mills.
CONCEIÇAO-D'APPODI, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-Grande-do-Norte, district of Porto- Alegre, extending along the W confines of the prov.

CONCEICAO-DA-SERRA, a parish and town of Brazil, in the prov. of Espirito-Santo, 10 m. NW of Victoria, bounded by the ocean on the E, and on the S by the Cururipe. Pop. 1,500.—Also a village in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, 25 m. N of Cuiaba, inhabited by Indians of the Payaguas tribe. CONCEIÇAO-DAS-VARGENS-DO-APPODI, or Appodi, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio- Grande-do-Norte, district and 20 m. SE of Porto- Alegre, and about the same distance from the Serra Martins, watered by the Appodi. Pop. 1,200. The culture of cotton forms the chief industry of the locality.
CONCEIÇAO, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Paulo, and district of Atibaia.
CONCEICAO-DE-MERUO'CA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceará, district of Januaria or Sobral, in the Serra Meruóca. Pop. 600, chiefly agriculturists.

CONCEIÇAO-DE-NORUEGA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 25 m. NNE of Minas- Novas, in the serra of Quatro-Oitavas. Pop. 1,200. Gold-mining and agriculture form the chief branches of local industry.

CONCEICAO-D'ITAMARACA, a district and town of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, comarca of Goianna. The town lies on the W side of the island of Itamaraca. The pop. of the district, comprising the island of Itamaracá and the small adjacent islands, was returned by the census of 1815 at 12,000; but erroneously, it may be presumed. It has since greatly declined in importance. Brandy forms the chief article of local manufacture. See also ITAMARACA'.
CONCEICAO-DO-PARA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 10 m. SE of Pitangui, on the I. bank of the Para, and on the road from Goyaz.

CONCEIÇAO-DOS-PRADOS, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, adjacent to the district of São-Jose, 50 m. from Mariana. Pop. 5,000. Agriculture and the rearing of cattle form the chief employments of the inhabitants.

CONCEIÇAO-DO-SERRO, a district and village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca and 30 m. SE of Serro. The district has a pop. of about 8,000. Iron is mined here, and there are gold-washings in some quarters.
CONCELHO-DE-JALES, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, comarca and 14 m. NE of Villa-Real.
CONCENTAINA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 27 m. N of Alicant, on the declivity of the Sierra-de-Mairola. Pop. 5,972. It has an active trade in woollens and hosiery, and some brandy distilleries.

CONCEPCION, a province of Chili, lying between the Itata and Biobio rivers. Area 5,210 sq. m. It is, as far as natural advantages go, much richer than any other district of Chili. It possesses a delightful climate, a fertile soil capable of producing the finest wheat, vines, olives, and rich pasture, and is covered with extensive forests of valuable timber. It likewise contains coal in abundance, but of inferior quality, as well as freestone, and is furnished with excellent ports, and numberless small streams, besides being intersected by a large river navigable for upwards of 100 m. [Hall.] The soil, if attended to, yields an abundant return. Wheat, barley, Indian corn, beans, pease, potatoes, and arrow-root, grapes, apples, pears, currants, strawberries, and olives, are the common produce of the country. From the latter a fine oil is extracted; but the fruit is too rank to be eaten at table, except by the natives. The arrow-root is of good quality. In the ravines and moist places, the panque (Gunnesa scabra), a very useful root, grows luxuriantly and strong. The wheat returns from 80 to 100 fold. Its average price at the city of C. is 10 reals = 5s. 6d. for 216 lbs. weight. When Stevenson was in this country in 1804-20, 6 ships of 400 tons each annually sailed from Talcahuano with wheat cargoes to Lima, Guayaquil, Panama, and Chiloe. Several European shrubs and herbaceous plants grow in the vicinity of the city of C. more luxuriantly than in England; among these are hemlock, flax, chickweed, pimpernel, water-cresses, and a species of elder. The wines of this prov. were formerly much esteemed, and carried along the coast to the northward; but have greatly deteriorated, and in the seaport are much adulterated. There is a great variety of them, and in general they are very intoxicating. [Beechey.] The reptiles are few, and not venomous. The pop. consists of a few Spaniards, some white Creoles and Mestizoes, and a few slaves and Indians. The city of Concepcion, or Concepcion-de-Mo-

CHA, the cap. of the above prov., is situated near the r. bank of the Biobio, 7 m. above its mouth, in S lat. 36° 49′ 30″, W long. 73° 05′ 20″ [Beechey]; S lat. 36° 43′ 15", W long. 72° 54' [Ulloa]. It stands upon a low neck of loose alluvial land between the N side of the Biobio and the SE angle of the bay of C., and occupies about 1 sq. m. The river, when high, CON washes the threshold of the nearest houses [Beechey]. It has no defences, and is commanded by a range of hills close behind it. Its streets intersect each other at right angles; and its houses are mostly only one story in height in consequence of the great frequency of earthquakes; many are built entirely of adobes or unbaked bricks. A small stream called the Andalien runs through the city, and supplies a fine fountain in the principal square. C. was formerly a flourishing town, containing several good buildings, and upwards of 20,000 inhabitants. When Beechey visited it in 1825, the pop. was only 6,500; and in 1828 it appears to have sunk to about 3,000. When the Indians took part in the civil war as the allies of the royalists, this rich city was made the special object of their attack; the wealthiest inhabitants abandoned it, and only the dregs of the pop. remained to occupy its ruins. Its reviving prosperity was again checked by the dreadful earthquake in Feb. 1835, by which its massive cathedral and the greater part of the city were totally destroyed. C. is the residence of a bishop, suffragan of Lima, and of the military governor of the prov. Its manufactures and trade are said to be at present of little importance, but are gradually recovering from the desolating effects of civil war, and the still more terrific effects of physical convulsions. The produce of the interior is brought down upon rafts, which are broken up and sold for timber. There is a ferry over the river into the Indian country. The natives cross in punts; but though the extreme distance is only 1 m., they have such difficulty in stemming the current, and avoiding banks and shallows, that they are sometimes 11 hour in performing the passage. -C. was founded in 1763, about 12 years after the destruction of the old city of Penco by inundation and earthquake. The bay of C.,—sometimes called the bay of Talcahuano,—is one of the largest on the W coast of S America. It is "a large square inlet, open on the N, while its S and W sides are formed by a high promontory jutting out from the mainland, and bending into the shape of an elbow, each side being 3 or 4 leagues long." [Hall.] The land on the W side is very steep; on the E, it slopes from the mountains towards the sea with gentle undulations. The diam. of the space thus enclosed is about 5 m. Its mouth is divided into two channels by the island Quiriquina, which lies across it. The N entrance has 30 fath. at its commencement, and 11 fath. at its entrance into the Talcahuano anchorage. The tide rises 6 ft. 3 in. [Perouse.] There are three harbours in this bay. That of Talcahuano, close to the small fortified town of the same name, under the promontory on the SW angle, is the most secure from winds, and that in which ships generally lie. Full 12 fath. water are found in all parts of the bay within 3 m. of the beach; the holding-ground is excellent; and the bottom free from rocks.—Several villages are situated along the shore on both sides, but principally on the E.
CONCEPCION, a town of Bolivia, near the confluence of the Chacares and Iraibi rivers, in S lat. 13° 50'.—Also a mission in the Chiquitos territory, 160 m. SSE of the foregoing.
CONCEPCION-DE-LA-CHINA, a town of the La Plata states, in the prov. of Entre Rios, on the r. bank of the Uruguay, 155 m. N by E of Buenos Ayres.
CONCEPCION-DEL-PAO, a town of Venezuela, in the prov. of Barcelona, on the Macuros river, 40 m. above its influx into the Orinocco.
CONCEPCION (VILLA-REAL-DE-LA-), a town of Paraguay, on the l. bank of the Paraguay, 130 m. NNE of Ascencion.

CONCEPCION, a small island in the Bahama group, SE of San Salvador, 23 m. in length from N to S, and 13 m. broad at its widest part. Its S extremity is in N lat. 23° 48′ 46", W long. 75° 6'. It attains an elevation above sea-level of about 80 ft. It is surrounded with a dangerous reef.—Also a group of small rocky islets off the N coast of the dep. of Ystmo, in New Grenada, 38 m. E of Mandinga, opposite the mouth of a small river of the same name.

CONCEPCION (Point), a headland on the coast of Upper California, in N lat. 34° 22′, 110 m. WNW of Point San-Pedro, and 35 m. W by N of the town of Santa-Barbara. The land from this point trends N; and to the northward, fogs and mists prevail during the early part of the day for three-fourths of the year, but the country becomes better wooded and is better supplied with water. The currents off Point C. set to the N in the early spring months.

CONCEPCION (RANCHO-DE-LA-PURISSIMA), a mission and village in Upper California, in the presidio of Santa-Barbara. Several quicksilver mines of extraordinary richness have been recently opened up in the vicinity of this place.

CONCEPCION STRAIT, an interior channel in Patagonia, between the strait of Magalhaens and the gulf of Penas, separating Madre-de-Dios, and its island to the S, from the mainland. It commences at Cape Santiago, in S lat. 50° 45', and joins the Wide channel in 50° 05'.—King.

CONCEPTION BAY, a large inlet on the coast of Newfoundland. It is 53 m. deep from Point-de- Grates on the W side, and 38 m. from Cape St. Francis on the E, to Holyrood; and is about 20 m. broad. Its shores are bold and mountainous, and inhabited by a wealthy and enterprising fishing population amounting to 22,000. It is supposed that iron and minerals of several species exist in the mountains surrounding this bay.
CONCESIO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 4 m. N by W of Brescia, on the road to Gardone, on the l. bank of the Mella. Pop. 1,100.
CONCEVO, an island in the Zambezi river, about 12 m. above Quilimane. It is nearly 1 m. in length, flat, and thickly covered with mangroves.

CONCHAGUA, or San Carlos, a small port on the coast of Nicaragua, in Central America, on the W side of the NW arm of the gulf of Fonseca, sometimes called the gulf of C., 5 m. NW of Chicarene Point, and 40 m. SSE of San Miguel. The actual town, or rather Indian village, from which this port derives its name, is situated about 3 m. up the Amapala mountain, in a secluded spot, destitute of water. See Fonseca.

CONCHAGUITA, a small island in the gulf of Fonseca, on the S coast of Nicaragua, 2 m. NW of Manguera island, and 6 m. WSW of Tigre island. It is a little above 2 m. in length from NNW to SSE, and 11 m. broad; and rises to the height of 500 ft. above sea-level.

CONCHAS, a name borne by the most western deltoid branch of the Rio-das-Piranhas, in the Brazilian prov. of Rio-Grande-do-Norte.—Also a small bay in the prov. of Bahia, comarca of Porto-Seguro, into which the Mugiquiçaba river discharges itself.

CONCHAS (Las), a town of Buenos-Ayres, 24 m. NW of the city of Buenos-Ayres, on the r. bank of the Rio-de-la-Plata, at the mouth of a river of the same name, which, rising to the S of Lujan, has a prevailingly ENE course of about 60 m.
CONCHE'E (LA), an islet of France, dep. of Illeet-Vilaine, 23 m. N of Saint-Malo, in N lat. 48° 41' 2".

CONCHES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Eure, arrond. of Evreux.- CON in 1841.—The com. and town, 6 m. SW of Evreux, on the r. bank of the Iton, had a pop. of 2,074 in 1836. There are iron-works here, and a large iron foundry.
CONCHEZ, a town of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, cant. of Garlin, 20 m. NNE of Pau, near the r. bank of the Lies. Pop. 461.
CONCHOS, a town of Mexico, on the N frontier of Durango, near the r. bank of the Rio-San-Pable, 90 m. SSW of Chihuahua.

CONCHOS (RIO-DE-), a large river of Mexico, which has its source near Santa-Cruz, on the E flank of the Sierra Madre, in the prov. of Durango, in N lat. 28°; and, running from S to N through Durango and Chihuahua, falls into the Rio-del-Norte on the r. bank, in N lat. 30° 30', after a course of about 300 m. The Rio-San-Pablo joins it on the L bank, at Bakinao, after a course of about 150 m.; and the Rio-Florida, on the r. bank, after a nearly equal length of course.

CONCHUCOS, a district of Bolivia, in the NW extremity of the dep. of Junin, between the Tunguragua or Maranon, and the Andes. It is a rude mountainous district, in which many of the villages have an alt. of from 5,000 to 8,000 ft. above sea-level. The valleys are generally mere quebradas or ravines. Silver mines exist here, but hitherto have not been successfully wrought, probably on account of the extreme barbarity of the inhabitants. There are also lavadores or washing-places for gold along the margin of the river Miraflores; and sulphur is extensively wrought near Llamellin. The Indians of C. are celebrated for their manufacture of ponchos, some of which are valued at 150 dollars. The pop. of this district was estimated at 23,308 in 1820, of whom 9,899 were Indians, and 13,983 Mestizoes. Its cap. is Huari-del-Rey.
CONCHY-LES-POTS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Ressons. Pop. 951.
CONCISE, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 4 m. NNE of Granson, on the l. bank of the lake of Neufchatel. Pop. 704. The environs are chiefly laid out in vineyards.

CONCLUSION PORT, an inlet at the S extremity of George the Third's archipelago, in Russian America, in N lat. 56° 16', W long. 134° 23'. It is about 2 m. in length, and has a depth of 75 fath. in the middle. Vancouver's ship was moored here at the conclusion of his laborious survey of the W shore of North America.

CONCOBELLA, a town of Lower Guinea, in the Mosongo territory, on the r. bank of the Zaire, in S lat. 4° 15', about 240 m. direct distance from the mouth of the river, of which nothing is known beyond this point. The Jesuit missionaries who have visited this place describe it as a populous town, 3 m. in circumf.

CONCORD, a township in Somerset co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 54 m. N of Augusta, on the W side of the Kennebec river. Pop. 577.—Also the cap. of Merrimac co., and of the state of New Hampshire, situated on both sides of the Merrimac river, 65 m. NNW of Boston, in N lat. 43° 12′ 29". The statehouse is a handsome structure; and the state-prison is also situated here. The falls of the Merrimac create a vast water-power, which is turned to considerable advantage. By Concord river, which has been made navigable for boats, by the Middlesex canal, and by a railroad, communication is kept up with Boston. Pop. 4,897.—Also a township in Essex co., in the state of Vermont, 47 m. NE of Montpelier, on the W branch of the Connecticut. Pop. 1,024.—Also the semi-capital of Middlesex co., in Massachusetts, 17 m. NW of Boston, on Concord The cant., comprising 28 com., had a pop. of 11,987 river. Pop. 1,784.—Also a township in Erie co., in the state of New York, on Cattaraugus creek, 25 m. S of Buffalo. Pop. 3,021.—Also a v. in Sussex co., in the state of Delaware.—Also a v. in Franklin co., in the state of Pennsylvania.—Also a township in Erie co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 652.—Also a township in Delaware co., in Pennsylvania, 20 m. W of Philadelphia. Pop. 1,057.—Also a township in Lake co., in the state of Ohio, 177 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 1,136.—Also the cap. of Cabarrus co., in the state of N Carolina, on Big Cold-water creek. Pop. 350.—Also a v. in Tippecanoe co., in Iowa.—Also a v. in Baker co., in Georgia.—Also a v. in Lewis co., in Kentucky, on the Ohio river.—Also a township in Jackson co., in Michigan, 84 m. W by S of Detroit, on the Kalamazoo river. Pop. 814.—Also a v. in White co., Illinois.—Also a v. in Washington co., in Missouri. Pop. 597.—Also a v. in Green co., in Arkansas. Pop. 256.—Also a township in Ross co., in Ohio. Pop. 2,549.—Also a v. in Champaign co., in Ohio. Pop. 935.—Also a township in La Fayette co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,014.—Also a township in Miami co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,034.

CONCORDIA, a parish in the state of Louisiana, bounded by the Mississippi, Tensas, and Red rivers. Area 1,300 sq. m. The surface is level, and liable to inundation. The chief production is cotton. Pop. in 1841, 9,414. The cap. is Vidalia.—Also a v. in Harrison township, in Dark co., Ohio.—Also a v. in Fayette co., Tennessee.
CONCORDIA, a town of Italy, in the duchy and 18 m. N of Modena, on the r. bank of the Secchia. Pop. 3,000.
CONCORDIA-DI-QUA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 36 m. NE of Venice, on the r. bank of the Limene. Pop. 600. It gives title to a bishop, whose see has been transferred to Portogruaro.
CONCORDIA (FORT). See COEPANG.
CONCORE'S, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Bozouls, 7 m. NNE of Rodez. Pop. 748.—Also a com. in the dep. of Lot, 8 m. NNE of Gourdon. Pop. 1,300.
CONCORET, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Mauron. Pop. 1,157.
CONCRE'MIERS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Indre, cant. and 2 m. SW of Le Blanc, on the Anglin. Pop. 1,053.

CONDAMINE, a large river of Australia, rising about 10 m. S of Cunningham's gap, in the Great dividing range, under the parallel of 28° 5' S, and flowing NNW, in a direction parallel to the range in the lower part of its course. It appears to be a series of ponds and deep reaches, through which there is a perceptible current, and the banks of which are fringed with high reeds; and to empty itself into a lagune, having no apparent outlet, which lies 60 m. direct distance W by N from its head. Its ponds are separated from each other by necks of land of from a few yards to some miles in breadth, beneath which the water forces its way. It forms for a great distance the separation of the sandstone country to the W, from the rich basaltic plains to the E. [Leichardt.]

CONDAPILLY, or KONDA-PILLI, one of the five original Northern Circars of Hindostan, now included within the Masulipatam collectorship. Along with Ellore, it comprised the territory lying between the Kistna and the Godaveri. Its cap., of the same name, is situated in N lat. 16° 39', 55 m. NW of Masulipatam. See CIRCARS.

CONDAT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Pontaumur, 30 m. WSW of Riom. Pop. 2,034.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Uzerche, 18 m. NNW of Tulle. Pop. 1,675.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. | neighbouring mines. II. CON of Haute-Vienne, cant. and 8 m. from Limoges. Pop. 1,500.
CONDAT-EN-FENIERS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Marcenat, 20 m. NNW of Murat, on the r. bank of the Rue. Pop. 3,582 in 1836.
CONDAT-LES-MONTROISSIER, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Saint-Germain-l'Harm. Pop. 1,202.

CONDATCHY, or KONDACHE', a village in the island of Ceylon, 4 m. S of Aripo, consisting of a few huts on a semicircular bay in a waste sandy district, covered with stunted prickly jungle, but which during the pearl-fishing season becomes the principal rendezvous of the busy crowds engaged in the fishery, an extemporary town being reared for their accommodation of huts composed of two or three palmyra leaves supported on a few branches. See CEYLON, p. 425.
CONDAVIR, a town of Hindostan, in the N Circars, 15 m. SW of Guntur.

CONDE, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and comarca of Bahia, at the mouth of the Inhambupe. Sugar-canes, tobacco, and mandioc, are cultivated in the environs. Pop. 2,000.—Also a village in the prov. and 1 m. S of Parahiba, between the rivers Japoquinha and Japoca. Pop. 800, chiefly Indians.— Also a v. in the prov. of Para, 12 m. SE of Belem, on the E bank of the Tocantins, on an island formed by that river, and the Moju, and the canal or channel of Iguape-Mirim, which communicates with both.

CONDE', or CONDE'-SUR-L'ESCAUT, a canton and fortified town of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. of Valenciennes.—The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 18,993; in 1841 of 20,479.—The town, which is on the r. bank of the Scheldt, at the embouchure of the Haine, 25 m. SE of Lille, 6 m. NNE of Valenciennes, had a pop. in 1836 of 5,297. It has a communal college, and contains a handsome town-hall, and a fine arsenal. The manufacture of starch, chickory-coffee, and of soap, boat-building, bleacheries, and coal-works, afford employment to the inhabitants. C. is connected with Mons by a canal. It first came into the possession of France at the peace of Nimeguen in 1679; its works were subsequently strengthened; and it is now regarded as one of the strongest barrier-fortresses of France on the side of the Netherlands. In July 1794, it surrendered to the Austrians after a close blockade, but it was retaken by the French the following year. C. was one of the French fortresses occupied by the Allies in execution of the second treaty of Paris in 1815. The small lordship of this name gave title to a well-known branch of the house of Bourbon, which became extinct in the person of the unfortunate Duke d'Enghien.
CONDE'-EN-BARROIS, a village of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Vavincourt, 6 m. N of Bar-le-Duc. Pop. 1,376.

CONDE'-EN-BRIE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Chateau-Thierry. — The cant., comprising 27 com., had a pop. of 11,547 in 1841.—The com. and v. are 8 m. ESE of Chateau-Thierry, near the confluence of the Surmelin and the Dhuis. Pop. 692.
CONDE'-FOLIE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Picquigny. Pop. 1,135.
CONDE'-SAINT-LIBIE'RE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Crecy. Pop. 443.
CONDE' (VIEUX), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Condé, 8 m. N of Valenciennes. Pop. in 1831, 3,976. A large quantity of coal is shipped here, on the Scheldt, from the 2 s
CONDE'-SUR-HUINE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Remalard, on the Huine. Pop. 1,382.
CONDE'-SUR-ITON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Breteuil, on the 1. bank of the Iton, 18 m. SSW of Evreux. Pop. 1,133. There are iron-forges here.
CONDE'-SUR-LAIZON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Bretteville, 18 m. SE of Caen, on the Laizon. Pop. 423.

CONDE'-SUR-NOIREAU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Vire.—The caut. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,713; in 1841, 13,477.—The com. and town, 18 m. E of Vire, at the confluence of the Noireau and the Drouance, had a pop. of 5,485 in 1846. It is a place of active industry, having manufactories of linens, cottons, cotton yarn, leather, and cutlery; and a trade in cattle, horses, lint, flax, and honey.
CONDE'-SUR-VIRE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Torigni, on the r. bank of the Vire. Pop. 2,164.
CONDEAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Remalard, 19 m. SE of Mortagne, on the r. bank of the Huîne. Pop. 1,071.
CONDEIX-A-VELHA, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarea and 6 m. S of Coimbra. The environs are noted for their oranges, and contain quarries of excellent free-stone.
CONDE'ON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Baignes, 4 m. S of Barbezieux. Pop. 1,369.
CONDERTON, a hamlet in the p. of Overbury, Worcestershire, 7 m. from Tewkesbury. Pop. 131.

CONDESUYOS, a district of Peru, in the intendancy of Arequipa, extending northwards from the confines of Bolivia, between the district of Caillomas on the E, and Camana on the W; and intersected in a NE direction by the Val-de-Mayes, which is enclosed on either side by ramifications of the Cordillera-de-Huambo, and watered by the Camana. The valleys produce grain, wine, and cochined. climate of the higher parts is cold, but they afford good pasturage. Some of the gold mines are still wrought to advantage. The pop. in 1831 was 20,145, of whom 12,011 were Indians and 4,358 Creoles. The cap., Chiquibamba, is a small town, situated on the r. bank of the Camana.
CONDICOTE, a parish of Gloucestershire, 3 m. from Stow-on-the-Wold, and 9 m. from Campden. Area 890 acres. Pop. 165.
CONDINO, a large village of Tyrol, in the gov. of Innspruck, circle and 23 m. W of Roveredo, in a fertile valley, on the r. bank of the Chiese, 9 m. NNE of the entrance of that river into Lake Idro.
CONDOJANNE, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 6 m. SSW of Gerace, and 33 m. ESE of Reggio, on a hill. Pop. 800. It contains 3 parish churches and 2 convents.

CONDOM, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Gers.-The arrond., comprising an area of 148,358 hectares, contains 6 cant., viz.: Cazaubon, C., Eauze, Montreal, Nogaro, and Valence. Pop. in 1831, 71,487; in 1846, 72,222.—The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,488; in 1841, 13,002.—The town is pleasantly situated on the Baïse, in a fertile valley, 27 m. NNW of Auch, in N lat. 45° 57′ 49". Pop. in 1789, 3,172; in 1836, 7,098; and in 1846, 6,915. It contains several churches, a communal college, and 2 hospitals; but the only building worthy of note is the cathedral, a fine Gothic structure. The manufactures, consisting in calico, thread, porcelain, and pens, are considerable; there are also several dye-works, tanneries, spinning-mills, and wax-works. C. forms the CON chief entrepot for the wine and brandy of the surrounding country, and has also an active trade in corn.—This town owes its origin to an ancient monastery. It became the capital of the ancient district of Condomois and was erected into a bishopric in 1317. It suffered much during the religious wars in 1569.

CONDORCET, a village of France, in the dep. of the Drôme, cant. and 6 m. NE of Nions, on a lofty acclivity the summit of which is crowned with a castle. Pop. 626. Fairs are held three times a-year. In the environs are a mine of lead and quarries of gypsum.

CONDORE (Pulo), an island in the Indian sea, near the coast of Cochin-China, having its centre in N lat. 8° 40', E long. 106° 42', about 45 m. from the mouth of the W branch of the Cambodia river. It is about 12 m. in length, and 4 m. in greatest breadth; and consists chiefly of a range of high and rugged mountains, which appear to reach an elevation of 1,800 ft. Wherever the hills are exposed to the full influence of the SW or the NE monsoon, they are either altogether bare, or covered only with coarse grasses or herbaceous plants; but where there is shelter, the forest grows exuberantly. The geological structure of the island is primitive, consisting generally of sienitic and grey granite. The soil is barren, producing only a little maize and rice, sweet potatoes, beans, gourds, and water-melons. It is destitute of spring-water; and noxious reptiles are reported to abound. Of quadrupeds, Crawford and his party saw only monkeys and black squirrels. There is a good roadstead or harbour, formed by a channel between the great island and six islets situated opposite to it on the E coast. The inhabitants are chiefly Cochin-Chinese. Their principal occupation is catching turtle, fishing, and extracting dammer, a kind of pitch, and oil, from some species of forest-trees. Their number amounts to about 800. The English, in 1702, at the instigation of Dampier, attempted to form a settlement on this island, hoping to create an entrepot by which their goods might be The introduced into the ports of China; but the settlers were soon cut off by the treachery of the natives and of the garrison, composed of a few natives of Celebes, in 1804. There are several other small islands off the E coast of the great island, besides those by which the harbour is formed. Their general aspect is black, rugged, and precipitous. The Cochin-Chinese junks trading with Singapore touch at this island for wood and water.—The two words Pulo Condore signify, in Malay, 'the Island of Gourds.'
CONDOVE, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 12 m. E of Susa, near the l. bank of the Dora-Riparia. Pop. 1,200.
CONDOVER, a parish in Salop, 5 m. S of Shrewsbury. Area 10,540 acres. Pop. 1,550.

CONDRIEU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Rhône, arrond. of Lyons.- The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. of 10,804 in 1831; of only 10,108 in 1841.—The town is situated on the r. bank of the Rhone, 20 m. S of Lyons, at an alt. of 126 metres = 413 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 3,300. It is a poorly-built place; but has some silk manufactories, and conducts an active trade, by means of the river, in wine and grain. The Rhone is here crossed by large boats worked by ropes sustained by cranes on each side of the river.
CONE, a mountain in the Chonos archipelago, one of three in the peninsula of Tres Montes, in S lat. 46° 34′ 10″, W long. 75° 31′.
CONE BAY, a deep bay, 42 m. wide, on the E shore of King's sound, in N Australia. It has a barrier of reefs and islets across its mouth.—Stokes.
CONE POINT, a headland on the S coast of Tierra-del-Fuego, in S lat. 54° 06′ 35″, W long. 70° 51' 45".

CONECOCHEAGUE, a river of Pennsylvania, U. S., which, rising in the N skirts of Franklin co., and running SSW through that co. and Washington co., falls into the Potomac, on the l. bank, at Williamsport, after a course of 40 m.

CONECUH, a county in the S part of the state of Alabama, U. S, watered by the C. river and its branches. Area 1,531 sq. m. Pop. 8,197. Its chief production is cotton. Its cap. is Sparta.—The C. river rises in Alabama, in several streams; and runs SSW till it enters Florida, where it turns S, and, under the name of the Escambia, flows into the NW arm of Pensacola bay. It has a total length of course of about 200 m., and is navigable for about 100 m.
CONEDOGWINIT, a river of Pennsylvania, U. S., which waters Cumberland co., and falls into the Susquehanna 2 m. above Harrisburg, after a NW course of about 80 m.

CONEGLIANO, or CORNEGLIANO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 16 m. N of Treviso. Pop. in 1837, 6,031; in 1843, 6,459. It has silk and woollen manufactories. Marshal Moncey was created duke of C. by Napoleon.
CONEJERA, or CONEY ISLANDS, a group of uninhabited islets lying off the W coast of the island of Ivica. The largest is 11 m. in length from N to S.
CONEJOS, or ISLA-DE-CABRAS, a small island lying to the SW of Juan Fernandez, in the S Pacific.
CONELLO. See KONELLO.

CONEMAUGH, a township in Cambria co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 15 m. SW of Ebensburg. Pop. 1,228.—Also a township in Indiana co., in the same state. Pop. 1,141.—Also a river of Pennsylvania, which rises in the Alleghany mountains, and flows WNW into the Alleghany, at a point 29 m. NNE of Pittsburg, after a course of 150 m.

CONEQUENESSING, a township in Butler co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 10 m. W of Butler. Pop. 2,698.—Also a small stream in the same township, which flows into the Mahonning, 12 m. above its mouth.

CONESTOGA, a river of Pennsylvania, U. S., flowing SW through Lancaster co. to the Susquehanna, into which it falls 10 m. below Colombia.- Also a township on the C. river, 5 m. S of Lancaster. Pop. 2,886.
CONESUS, a township in Livingstone co, in the state of New York, U. S., 10 m. SE of Geneseo. Pop. 1,654.
CONESVILLE, a township in Schoharie co. in the state of New York, U. S., 42 m. SW of Albany. Pop. 1,621.

CONEWAGO, a township in York co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,068.—Also a river in Adam's co., in the same state, which falls into the Susquehanna, opposite to Bainbridge, after a course of 40 m.—Also a township in Adam's co., on the C. river. Pop. 899.

CONEWANGO, a township in Cattaragus co., in the state of New York, 316 m. W by S of Albany. Pop. 1,317.—Also a stream flowing from Chatauque lake through C. township, in the state of New York, into the Alleghany.—Also a township in Warren co., in the state of Pennsylvania, skirted by Alleghany river on the S. Pop. 858.
CONEY, or CUNY, an island off the coast of co. Sligo, 4 m. WNW of the town of Sligo. It is 33 m. in circumference.

CONEY, a river of France, which rises 4 m. S of Epinal, in the dep. of Vosges; passes Fontenois-le- Chateau, and enters the dep. of Haute-Saone; and flows into the Saone, on the l. bank, after a course from NE to SW of 36 m., of which 9 m. from Selles CON downwards are navigable. Its principal affluent is a small stream flowing from the etang called Void-de- Cone.
CONEYSTHORPE, a township in the p. of Barton-in-the-Street, N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. W of New Malton. Area 1,150 acres. Pop. 170.
CONEYTHORPE, a township in the p. of Goldsborough, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. E by N of Knaresborough. Area 800 acres. Pop. 104.
CONFIENZA, a village of Piedmont, in the div. and 9 m. SSW of Novara, prov. of Lomellina, mandamento and 5 m. NW of Robbio. Pop. 1,593.

CONFLANS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Moselle, arrond. of Briev. The cant. comprises 24 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,494; in 1841, 9,101. The town is situated at the confluence of the Iron with the Orne, 8 m. S of Briey. Pop. in 1846, 440. Fairs are held here twice a-year. -Also a com. and town in the dep. of the Haute- Saône, cant. of Saint-Loup, 18 m. NW of Lure, on the l. bank of the Semouse. Pop. 841. Near the town are some iron-mines.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. and 3 m. N of Saint-Calais, on the Anille. Pop. 1,156. It contains a manufactory of serge.—Also a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Upper Savoy, 24 m. ENE of Chambery, at the confluence of the Arli with the Isere, and opposite l'Hopital. Pop. 2,000. It stands on a hill at an alt. of 1,174 ft. above sea-level, and was formerly a place of considerable strength. It contains a church, a college, and two convents. Its chief importance now arises from the adjacent mines of argentiferous lead, the produce of which annually amounts to 8 quintals of silver. This town is supposed by some to occupy the place of the ancient Oblimum of the Romans. It was taken and dismantled by Francis I. in 1536.

CONFLANS-L'ARCHEVEQUE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. and com. of Charenton-le-Pont, 4 m. SE of Paris, on the r. bank of the Seine, below the embouchure of the Marne. Pop. 100. It contains a castle, belonging to the archbishops of Paris.

CONFLANS-SAINTE-HONORINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Poissy, 16 m. N of Versailles, on the r. bank of the Seine, near the confluence of the Oise. Pop. 1,634. It contains a copper and tin finery, and a foundry of bronze and brass. In the vicinity is a fine castle and park; and, on an adjacent mountain, the remains of two ancient fortresses.
CONFLENS, a village of France, in the dep. of Ariège, cant. of Oust, on the Salat, 16 m. S of St. Girons. Pop. 1,055.
CONFLENTI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 8 m. N of Nicastro. Pop. 2,000. Lint and silk are extensively cultivated in the environs.

CONFOLENS, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Charente.—The arrond., comprising an area of 141,836 hectares, contains 5 cant., viz.: Chabannais, Champagne-Mouton, Saint Claud, Confolens, and Montembouf. Pop. in 1846, 70,846.—The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 21,098; in 1841, 21,256.—The town is situated on the r. bank of the Vienne, at the confluence of the Goire, and on the road from Limoges to Nantes, 39 m. NE of Angouleme. Pop. in 1789, 1,378; in 1821, 2,522; in 1836, 2,766; and in 1846, 2,765. It contains an ancient bridge, a communal college, and a public library. It is badly built, and of its ancient fortress nothing but a tower now exists. It has, however, an active trade in timber, staves, cattle, and grain. Fairs are held monthly, and are numerously attended. In the environs are mines of argentiferous lead, sulphur of zinc, and cadmium.
CONFOY, a parish of co. Kildare, 25 m. WNW of 1 Leixlip. Area 1,129 acres. Pop. in 1841, 135.
CONFRANCON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ain, cant. of Montrevel, 10 m. NW of Bourg. Pop. 1,324.

CONGA, a parish, partly in co. Galway, partly in co. Mayo. Area of the Galway section 22,840 acres, of which 11,764 are in Lough Corrib. Area of the Mayo section 14,888 acres, of which 1,885 are in Lough Corrib, and 1,292 in Lough Mask. Pop. of the whole in 1831, 8,378; in 1841, 8,835. The W part of this large p. rises into the lofty mountain-range of Joyce- Country. The E part is level and marshy.—In the Mayo section stands the small town of C., 5 m. S by W of Ballinrobe. Pop. 364.

CONGAREE, a river of the state of South Carolina, U. S., formed by the junction of Broad and Saluda rivers at Columbia. After a course of 30 m. it unites with the Wateree, and takes the name of Santee river.
CONGERSTON, a parish of Leicestershire, 3 m. NW of Market-Bosworth, on a branch of the Anker, and near the Ashby-de-la Zouch canal. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 267.
CONGHAM, a parish of Norfolk, 3 m. ESE of Castle-Rising. Area 3,260 acres. Pop. 326.

CONGLETON, a chapelry, market-town, and municipal borough, in the p. of Astbury, co.-palatine of Chester, 71 m. SSW of Macclesfield, pleasantly situated in a picturesque valley on the S bank of the Dane, on the London and Manchester road, and in the vicinity of the Macclesfield canal. It was intended that the Manchester and Birmingham railway should pass through Congleton and the Potteries to Stone; but a viaduct in course of erection here, was abandoned in consequence of the intended junction of this line at Crewe with the Grand Junction railway. Area of the chapelry 2,500 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,861; in 1831, 9,352; in 1841, 9,222. It consists of narrow streets, and the houses are irregularly built. There are cotton-spinning factories here; but the principal manufacture is confined to the silk trade. Powerloom weaving is the staple weaving trade; hand-loom weaving is now of comparatively limited amount. A silk mill was first established here about 1752. There were, in 1839, 100 engine-weaving hand-looms, possessed by about the same number of weavers, and 254 power-looms, possessed by 7 manufacturers. Looms for weaving broad-silks were, at one time, very numerous; but they are mostly moved to Macclesfield; ribbon-looms are chiefly worked here.

CONGO, a name formerly given to the whole maritime districts of SW Africa, lying between the parallels of 2° and 16° S., but now restricted to the territory between the Zaire or Congo river on the N, which separates it from Loango; and the Dando river, which separates it from Angola, on the S. It is bounded on the E by a country very little known and inhabited by savage tribes, which appears to rise into a long ridge of mountains running in a direction parallel to the coast, and ascending from the littoral region in a series of terraces. Its greatest length from NW to SE is about 200 m., and its breadth about 150 The breadth widens inland with the course of m. the Zaire which winds above 2° more to the N. Numerous rivers descend from the mountains on the E, Dando appear to be the most considerable. Among these mountains also lies the lake of Akilunda, under the parallel of 7° 30' S. The coast and level littoral region of C. is unhealthy, on account of its low grounds and forests; but the interior has a temperate climate, and is described by the missionaries as populous and wellcultivated. The soil produces an immense variety of plants, and tropical fruits. Several kinds of grain unknown in Europe are cultivated near the rivers; among them is the luke or lune, from which a very fine white bread is made; but rice and maize are the most common grains. Three crops of maize are reaped annually. C. might, by industry, be made productive of every kind of grain except wheat, for which the ground is represented as being too fertile. Some kinds of pease and beans are reared; but the inhabitants enervated by a warm climate, and a country which is fertile without culture, are said to bestow little attention on the cultivation of the ground. Agricultural operations are carried on with a rude hoe stuck into a wooden handle; yet with the slight scratching which the ground receives from this rude instrument, it produces excellent crops. The rights of property are strictly recognised; and the division is sometimes so minute, that three or four persons will have a fowl or a pig betwixt them. Among the trees, the baobab is mentioned as attaining here an enormous size. Its fruit is eaten by the natives. What of the country was observed by Captain Tuckey, was occupied by very small villages, and far from being highly improved. The banks of the Zaire, as far as Embomma, are alluvial, and covered with luxuriant verdure chiefly of natural growth. Higher up are bare mountains, from 2,000 to 3,000 ft. in height, composed chiefly of mica-slate, quartz, and sienite; the villages and cultivated spots are situated in the ravines. Farther up still, the mountains open and allow the river to flow in a wider channel; they are composed here of limestone and clay; and the greater part of the surface is fit for cultivation. The oilpalm, Elæis Guiniensis, is common to this country, with all Western Africa. The collection of plants formed by Professor Smith, in the late expedition, consisted of 620 species, of which about 250 were absolutely new. The luxuriance of the vegetation of C. is said to be eminently displayed, in the great numbers of beautiful flowers which adorn every meadow; and-what is somewhat uncommon in the flowers of a warm country—these flowers are described as emitting a fragrance no less grateful to the smell, than their colours are beautiful to the eye. Cavazzi speaks of tulips of an extraordinary brilliancy of colour, and fragrancy of odour. He also mentions lilies superior both in colour and smell to those of Europe. Tuberoses, hyacinths, and many other kinds of flowers are represented as here attaining, in a wild state, a beauty and perfection which culture cannot bestow in other regions. It is confessed, however, that many kinds of exotic flowers and plants degenerate in C., unless they have the advantage of continual and careful cultivation.—There are two seasons,—the dry and the rainy. The latter, which begins near the Zaire in October, and ends in April, is accompanied by rain, thunder, and tempest.—In the mountains are found iron, copper, porphyry, jasper, marble, salt, crystal, gold, and silver. C., like the rest of Guinea, abounds in wild animals. The elephant, lion, leopard, zebra, boar, jackal, gazelle, and antelope, with a great variety of apes, are the principal. The rivers are frequented by hippopotami, crocodiles, and turtles. No domestic animals are employed in draught or agriculture; but for food there is a considerable supply of goats, hogs, fowls, ducks, and pigeons. Sheep and of which the Bengo, the Ambriz, the Lifanhe, and the | cattle are scarce. The lower part of the river abounds with excellent fish, particularly bream, mullet, catfish, and sparus, the latter often weighing from 30 to 60 pounds, and of excellent flavour. Among the reptiles, which are numerous, are the gigantic boa, the chamelion, and the flying lizard or palm-rat. The latter is worshipped by the natives. The deserts and forests are inhabited by ostriches, peacocks, parrots, and a variety of other birds. A great number of noxious insects are found here, such as the banzo, the sting of which is said to be mortal; and the insondi, which penetrates the trunk of the elephant, causing madness and death. Mosquitos and ants abound; bees also are numerous, and furnish delicious honey. The domestic animals, brought here by the Portuguese, thrive well.-Although this country abounds in all the productions of the tropics, there seems to be little commerce, except in slaves, who are carried hence in vast numbers to Brazil. European merchants chiefly reside at San Salvador. The imports are either from Europe or Brazil. From Europe they receive English cloth, stuffs, and Turkey carpets, brass vessels, copper, and blue earthen ware; ornaments, and rings of gold, silver, and other metals; light stuffs of cotton, woollen, and linen, glass beads, coral, bugles, and other trinkets, with a few tools and utensils. From Brazil are imported fruits, grain, and different kinds of American produce. The exports formerly consisted of ivory, furs, and such articles as the country affords; but the trade was confined chiefly to slaves. This abominable traffic was nominally put an end to by the treaty between Great Britain and Portugal in 1821. The pop. of C. cannot be accurately stated; the missionaries and other travellers differ in their statements considerably. The Portuguese missionaries represented C. as being extremely populous. San Salvador, the capital, they affirmed, contained no fewer than 50,000 inhabitants. One single province, Bamba, was said to have furnished an army of 200,000 men; and, in 1665, the king of C.'s army is said by Carli to have amounted to 900,000 men. This immense pop. was represented as subsisting without diminution, notwithstanding the annual exportation of 15,000 or 16,000 slaves. Such an account is evidently exaggerated. The fact is, that the prince who can muster 200 soldiers, and arm the half of them with muskets, becomes the terror of all this part of Africa. These little chiefs, however, acknowledge a certain supremacy in the Blindy N'Congo, or general sovereign of the country, who resides at a town called Congo, situated about six days' journey in the interior, probably the San Salvador of the Portuguese. The natives of C. are of the middle size; their colour and features are less strongly marked than those of other Negroes; and they appeared to Captain Tuckey to be a mixed race. They are indolent, and less intelligent than many other Negro tribes; yet their features are more pleasing. Polygamy exists among them, and adultery is severely punished. They kill a number of slaves over the grave of their sovereign, who are intended to serve him in the other world. They worship fetiches, with which they cover themselves; and adore images, in which a resemblance to the Egyptian physiognomy seems to have been discovered. Murder is punished by death; and all other crimes by slavery. Society in C. may be divided into the following classes: 1st, The chenu, or chief, and his family. This dignity is hereditary in the female line; none of the sons of the chenu can inherit, unless the mother be of royal blood. The chenu is little distinguished from his subjects by dress or accommodation; his ensign of office consists of a small staff of black wood inlaid with lead or copper. The daughter of the chenu chooses her own husband, of whom she is absolute mistress, and whom she can even sell into slavery; so that the royal alliance is little an object of envy. 2d, The mafuks, or collectors of the revenue, who engage also in trade. 3d, The fumus, or yeomanry, who have houses and lands of their own; two or three wives, and perhaps a slave or two, who work for them. 4th, Fishermen and labourers, who having no property of their own, work | dinia, in the SW part of Piedmont; bounded on the CON for hire, and are much at the disposal of the chief. 5th, Domestic slaves. They are said not to be numerous, and are not considered as transferable property unless guilty of some serious offence. The slaves sold to Europeans are chiefly brought from the interior.—The language spoken in C. is said to be related to that of the Caffres and Bechuanas on the E side of the continent. The kingdom of C. is divided into several provinces, of which, under the Portuguese sway, there were six principal ones: Bamba, Batta, Pango, Pemba, Sindi, and Sonho, governed by chiefs, ruling under the Portuguese, with the titles of duke, count, and marquis. Each prov. had its banza or capital. At present almost every township has its own chenu or chief, who holds, as feudal lawyers would say, under a sovereign superior. Banza-Congo is the cap. of the whole kingdom.—C. was discovered by the Portuguese in 1487, under command of Diego Cam, who ascended the river Zaire. Soon after his return, the Portuguese government, partly by force, and partly by stratagem, took possession of the country, built forts along the coast, and sent missionaries to convert the people. They were successful for a time, and there are still a considerable number of catholics in the country.—Our only recent and reliable information respecting this country is to be gleaned from the imperfect papers of the unfortunate expedition to the Zaire; and the narrative of Captain Owen's surveying voyages. Douville's Voyage au Congo is a mere romance. See articles Ambriz, Angola, Ben-
GUELA, and ZAIRE.
CONGO. See ZAIRE.
CONGO, a small town of New Grenada, in the dep. of Ystmo, 40 m. SSW of Carreto, on a stream flowing into the gulf of San-Miguel.
CONGONHAS, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca of Serro-Frio, 30 m. E of Serra. Pop. 300.—Also a small stream in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, an affluent of the Santa-Anna.
CONGONHAS-DE-SABARA', a district and village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 5 m. SE of Sabará.
